Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 23 an inert, nonpenetrating and noninteractive solvent [8, 9] . More recent studies suggest that it is absorbed into the upper layers of the skin [10] . MG is composed of mixed triglycerides of fractionated C 8 –C 10 fatty acids derived from coconut oil. This compound has a wide variety of cosmetic and pharmaceutical applications [11, 12] but its effects on skin permeation are difficult to elucidate as it is typically employed in multicomponent preparations. To our knowledge, there is only one report in the litera- ture concerning the effects of medium chain triglycerides on skin permeation of a model hydrophilic drug [13] . A secondary aim of the present study was to investigate any correlation between ibuprofen transport in silicone and skin for the range of solvents studied to date. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. MO was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (UK) and MG from Sasol (Witten, Germany). The silicone mem- branes were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a mea- sured thickness of 400  m. All other materials were of reagent grade and were from VWR (Lutterworth, UK). Methods Solvent Uptake Studies Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was ob- tained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated as reported previ- ously [4] and stored in a freezer until required. The uptake of ve- hicles into silicone membrane and skin (dried for 24 h to dry weight over silica before use) was experimentally determined by a gravimetric method. The membrane was cut to an appropriate size (–1 cm 2 samples) and weighed using a balance (Sartorius, Ep- som, UK). The samples were then soaked in the vehicle for 24 h. The membranes were blotted dry with tissue paper and reweighed. Experiments were performed in triplicate, at room temperature. The amount of solvent taken up by the membrane was expressed as a weight percent. Solubility parameters for the selected vehicles were calculated using Hansen’s approach [14] . Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and an- alysed for drug content by HPLC or UV spectroscopy as described previously [4] . Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with standard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Reproducibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; the CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to the appropriate size. Permeation experiments were con- ducted with saturated solutions of ibuprofen for each vehicle us- ing Franz diffusion cells with dimensions previously reported [4] . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink conditions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experiments were performed at 32 ° C under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compart- ment with microscope cover slips. The number of replicates was at least 4. Flux values were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The perme- ability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fit- ting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [15] . 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h      d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±  (1) where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between all values were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05. Results and Discussion Solubility Table 1 illustrates the solubility of ibuprofen in MO, MG and various binary mixtures of the solvents. The higher solubility of ibuprofen in MG relative to MO ( fig. 1 ) reflects the similarity in their respective solubility parameters ( table 2 ); the calculated values are 9.4 (cal/ cm 3 ) 1/2 for MG, 7.1 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 for MO, and ibuprofen has a reported value of 9.7 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [16] . Similar trends were observed for ibuprofen solubility in water, ethanol and PG, i.e. the closer the solubility parameter between the solute and solvent, the higher the solubility. However, with increasing amounts of MG, the solubility of ibupro- fen in MG/MO systems does not increase in the exponen- tial manner observed for ethanol/water and PG/water mixtures [4, 5] . Solvent Uptake The uptake of selected vehicles into silicone mem- brane and skin is reported in table 2 and the value for water uptake is also included for comparison. The solu- bility parameter of silicone membrane is reported as 7.5 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [17] and the vehicles with values closest to this are taken up to a significantly (p ! 0.05) greater extent in silicone than MG and water. Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 23 an inert, nonpenetrating and noninteractive solvent [8, 9] . More recent studies suggest that it is absorbed into the upper layers of the skin [10] . MG is composed of mixed triglycerides of fractionated C 8 –C 10 fatty acids derived from coconut oil. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. MO was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (UK) and MG from Sasol (Witten, Germany). The silicone mem- branes were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a mea- sured thickness of 400  m. All other materials were of reagent grade and were from VWR (Lutterworth, UK). The epidermis was separated as reported previ- ously [4] and stored in a freezer until required. The uptake of ve- hicles into silicone membrane and skin (dried for 24 h to dry weight over silica before use) was experimentally determined by a gravimetric method. The membrane was cut to an appropriate size (–1 cm 2 samples) and weighed using a balance (Sartorius, Ep- som, UK). The samples were then soaked in the vehicle for 24 h. The membranes were blotted dry with tissue paper and reweighed. Experiments were performed in triplicate, at room temperature. The amount of solvent taken up by the membrane was expressed as a weight percent. Solubility parameters for the selected vehicles were calculated using Hansen’s approach [14] . Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 23 an inert, nonpenetrating and noninteractive solvent [8, 9] . More recent studies suggest that it is absorbed into the upper layers of the skin [10] . MG is composed of mixed triglycerides of fractionated C 8 –C 10 fatty acids derived from coconut oil. This compound has a wide variety of cosmetic and pharmaceutical applications [11, 12] but its effects on skin permeation are difficult to elucidate as it is typically employed in multicomponent preparations. To our knowledge, there is only one report in the litera- ture concerning the effects of medium chain triglycerides on skin permeation of a model hydrophilic drug [13] . A secondary aim of the present study was to investigate any correlation between ibuprofen transport in silicone and skin for the range of solvents studied to date. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. MO was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (UK) and MG from Sasol (Witten, Germany). The silicone mem- branes were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a mea- sured thickness of 400  m. All other materials were of reagent grade and were from VWR (Lutterworth, UK). Methods Solvent Uptake Studies Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was ob- tained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated as reported previ- ously [4] and stored in a freezer until required. The uptake of ve- hicles into silicone membrane and skin (dried for 24 h to dry weight over silica before use) was experimentally determined by a gravimetric method. The membrane was cut to an appropriate size (–1 cm 2 samples) and weighed using a balance (Sartorius, Ep- som, UK). The samples were then soaked in the vehicle for 24 h. The membranes were blotted dry with tissue paper and reweighed. Experiments were performed in triplicate, at room temperature. The amount of solvent taken up by the membrane was expressed as a weight percent. Solubility parameters for the selected vehicles were calculated using Hansen’s approach [14] . Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and an- alysed for drug content by HPLC or UV spectroscopy as described previously [4] . Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with standard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Reproducibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; the CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to the appropriate size. Permeation experiments were con- ducted with saturated solutions of ibuprofen for each vehicle us- ing Franz diffusion cells with dimensions previously reported [4] . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink conditions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experiments were performed at 32 ° C under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compart- ment with microscope cover slips. The number of replicates was at least 4. Flux values were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The perme- ability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fit- ting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [15] . 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h      d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±  (1) where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between all values were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05. Results and Discussion Solubility Table 1 illustrates the solubility of ibuprofen in MO, MG and various binary mixtures of the solvents. The higher solubility of ibuprofen in MG relative to MO ( fig. 1 ) reflects the similarity in their respective solubility parameters ( table 2 ); the calculated values are 9.4 (cal/ cm 3 ) 1/2 for MG, 7.1 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 for MO, and ibuprofen has a reported value of 9.7 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [16] . Similar trends were observed for ibuprofen solubility in water, ethanol and PG, i.e. the closer the solubility parameter between the solute and solvent, the higher the solubility. However, with increasing amounts of MG, the solubility of ibupro- fen in MG/MO systems does not increase in the exponen- tial manner observed for ethanol/water and PG/water mixtures [4, 5] . Solvent Uptake The uptake of selected vehicles into silicone mem- brane and skin is reported in table 2 and the value for water uptake is also included for comparison. The solu- bility parameter of silicone membrane is reported as 7.5 (cal/cm 3 ) 1/2 [17] and the vehicles with values closest to this are taken up to a significantly (p ! 0.05) greater extent in silicone than MG and water.
